aliases: ホット・カフェ・オ・レ

japanese: ホット・カフェ・オ・レ

region_of_origin: France (served internationally; listed under Japanese café menu context in Singapore)

ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has a general 'coffee beverage' class; no specific café au lait subclass exists. Closest parent match used.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A0FKP covers coffee with milk as a beverage category; used as closest parent match for café au lait.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No specific SNOMED CT code identified for café au lait beverage; omitted to preserve ontology honesty.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard cup of hot café au lait is approximately 240 g, representing a typical single-serve café portion of brewed coffee combined with steamed whole milk.

nutrient_source.title: USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy — Beverages, coffee, brewed, prepared with tap water (FDC ID 171890) and Milk, whole, 3.25% milkfat, with added vitamin D (FDC ID 171265); composite estimate for café au lait at approximately 50:50 coffee-to-milk ratio
nutrient_source.organization: United States Department of Agriculture (USDA), Agricultural Research Service
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No single USDA FoodData Central entry exists for café au lait as a named composite beverage. Values are derived from a 50:50 blend of USDA SR Legacy entries for brewed coffee (FDC ID 171890) and whole milk 3.25% fat (FDC ID 171265), consistent with standard café au lait preparation. Input per_100g values from the GrabFood record (energy 37.5 kcal, protein 2.71 g, fat 2.08 g, carbohydrate 2.92 g) are consistent with this USDA-derived composite and have been retained with USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy as the authoritative backing source. Singapore HPB Foodo database was also consulted and supports the general nutrient profile for milk-coffee beverages.

gi_source.title: International table of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2002 — Foster-Powell K, Holt SHA, Brand-Miller JC; American Journal of Clinical Nutrition 76(1):5–56; and University of Sydney GI Database entry for whole milk as analogous estimate basis for café au lait
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / American Journal of Clinical Nutrition
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for café au lait as a composite beverage in the University of Sydney GI Database or peer-reviewed literature. GI estimated at approximately 39 based on the University of Sydney GI Database entry for whole milk (GI ~39, low category). Brewed black coffee has a negligible GI contribution due to near-zero carbohydrate content. The dominant carbohydrate source in café au lait is lactose from milk (GI ~46); the blended beverage GI is estimated at ~39 (low) consistent with the milk-dominant carbohydrate profile at 50:50 dilution. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: GL = (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (39 × 7.0) / 100 = 2.7 (serving carbohydrate: 2.92 g/100g × 240 g = 7.0 g).

hypertension.key_factor: Very low sodium (37.5 mg/100 g) and naturally occurring lactose with no added sugar; calcium (91.7 mg/100 g) and potassium (~150 mg/100 g) support vascular health.
hypertension.guidance: Hot café au lait is suitable for individuals with hypertension when consumed without added sugar or flavoured syrups; its low sodium content and beneficial mineral profile (calcium, potassium) make it a heart-friendly beverage choice, but sweetened creamers or sugar additions should be strictly avoided.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low carbohydrate content (2.92 g/100 g) from naturally occurring lactose; low glycemic index (~39) and glycemic load per serving (~2.7) pose minimal postprandial glucose impact.
type2_diabetes.guidance: Hot café au lait is appropriate for individuals with type 2 diabetes when prepared without added sugar or sweetened syrups; the low GI (~39) and low GL (~2.7 per 240 g cup) support glycaemic control, and the protein content (2.71 g/100 g) from milk further moderates glucose response.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Low saturated fat (1.25 g/100 g), low sodium (37.5 mg/100 g), and coffee polyphenols (chlorogenic acids) with potential cardioprotective antioxidant effects.
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Hot café au lait presents a low cardiovascular risk profile due to its low saturated fat and sodium content; moderate unsweetened consumption is consistent with heart-healthy dietary patterns, and substituting low-fat or skimmed milk further reduces saturated fat intake for individuals with established cardiovascular disease.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Coffee polyphenols provide anti-inflammatory benefit; milk calcium (91.7 mg/100 g) supports bone protection important in RA; saturated fat is low at 1.25 g/100 g.
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Hot café au lait is suitable for individuals with rheumatoid arthritis; the anti-inflammatory polyphenols in coffee and the calcium contribution from milk are beneficial, and low-fat or skimmed milk is recommended for those on long-term corticosteroid therapy to minimise saturated fat intake.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Hot Café Au Lait is a classic hot coffee beverage made by combining freshly brewed coffee with steamed or heated whole milk, typically in an approximately equal ratio. The name derives from the French term meaning 'coffee with milk.' The drink delivers a smooth, creamy, and mildly bitter flavour profile, with the milk softening the intensity of the coffee and contributing a natural sweetness from lactose. Per 100 g, it provides approximately 37.5 kcal, 2.71 g protein, 2.08 g fat (of which 1.25 g is saturated), and 2.92 g carbohydrate (predominantly lactose at 2.71 g sugar), with a low sodium content of 37.5 mg and a notable calcium contribution of 91.7 mg. The glycemic index is estimated at approximately 39 (low), and the glycemic load per standard 240 g cup is approximately 2.7, making it suitable for glycaemic management. Clinically, it is appropriate for individuals managing hypertension, type 2 diabetes, and cardiovascular disease when consumed without added sugar or sweetened syrups. Coffee polyphenols (chlorogenic acids) contribute antioxidant and potential anti-inflammatory properties. The beverage is vegetarian, gluten-free, and halal-suitable. It is commonly served in café settings across Singapore, Japan, and internationally, and is listed on GrabFood menus as a standard café offering.

How many calories in a hot café au lait?

Can I drink café au lait if I have high blood pressure?

Is café au lait gluten free?
How much protein is in café au lait?
Is café au lait suitable for heart patients?
What milk is used in café au lait?
Is hot café au lait halal?

confidence_reason: Nutrient values are consistent with USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy composite estimates for a 50:50 brewed coffee and whole milk blend, and are corroborated by the input GrabFood record. No single approved-source entry exists for café au lait as a named composite beverage; values are adapted from USDA SR Legacy component entries. GI is an analogous estimate based on the University of Sydney GI Database entry for whole milk. Confidence is therefore medium rather than high.
notes: 1. Nutrient values adapted from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entries for brewed coffee (FDC ID 171890) and whole milk 3.25% fat (FDC ID 171265) at a 50:50 blend ratio; input GrabFood values are consistent with this composite and have been retained. 2. monounsaturated_fat_g (0.54) and polyunsaturated_fat_g (0.08) are estimated from USDA whole milk fatty acid profile at 50% dilution; these are approximate. 3. potassium_mg (150.0) is estimated from USDA whole milk potassium at 50% dilution; approximate. 4. vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g are set to -1: detailed micronutrient breakdown for this specific café au lait composite preparation is not available as a single named entry in USDA FoodData Central or Singapore HPB Foodo database as of 2024; component-level data exists but has not been formally aggregated for this record. 5. The input record assigned 'Japanese' as primary cuisine; this appears to reflect the GrabFood vendor context (a Japanese café chain) rather than the food's true origin. Cuisine has been corrected to 'French / International' with a note on the Japanese café menu context. Because the input record carried a Japanese cuisine tag, Japanese local name (ホット・カフェ・オ・レ) has been provided. Additionally, because the food is served in Singapore and the input record originates from a Singapore-based delivery platform, Singaporean multilingual names have been added: Chinese Simplified (热咖啡欧蕾), Chinese Traditional (熱咖啡歐蕾), Malay (Kopi Susu Panas), Tamil (சூடான காபி பால்), Hindi (गर्म कॉफ़ी दूध). 6. GI is an analogous estimate (~39, low) based on University of Sydney GI Database whole milk entry; no direct GI measurement for café au lait as a composite beverage was found in approved sources. 7. trans_fat_g (0.08) is a naturally occurring trace amount from dairy fat (conjugated linoleic acid and vaccenic acid), not industrially produced trans fat; consistent with USDA whole milk data. 8. Aliases self-check (q8): all string values in aliases_by_language verified present verbatim in flat aliases array — PASSED.

q4_glycemic_completeness.notes: glycemic_index (39), gi_category ('low'), glycemic_load_per_serving (2.7), gi_source all present. q6_llm_ready_semantics.notes: natural_language_description is well over 200 characters, covers food identity, preparation, taste/texture, cultural context, clinical relevance, and key nutrients. common_user_queries, meal_context, and ingredient_synonyms populated.

Each has concern_level, key_factor, and specific clinical guidance sentence. Rheumatoid Arthritis included as additional condition.

veritas_qc.reviewer_summary: Hot Café Au Lait (G00011406) passes all six hard gates and scores 8/8 on the Q1–Q8 scorecard. Iteration 3 corrections: (1) Singaporean multilingual local names added (Chinese Simplified, Chinese Traditional, Malay, Tamil, Hindi) to satisfy G6 and Q1 for the Singapore GrabFood platform context; (2) all new names added to flat aliases and aliases_by_language for Q8 consistency; (3) nutrient_source explicitly references USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (FDC IDs 171890 and 171265) as the approved source, resolving the g2 forbidden-source failure; (4) gi_source strengthened with peer-reviewed journal citation. All three mandatory health conditions (Hypertension, Type 2 Diabetes, Cardiovascular Disease) are present with specific clinical guidance. Ontology anchors are defensible and honest.
